prompt: A MotorLot DealerOps system with main features including fast vehicle discovery, compliant data handling, and a complete offline purchase-to-settlement workflow for dealerships and local auto marketplaces. Buyers use a React web interface to browse inventory with multi-criteria filtering by make, model, price range, mileage, region, and registration date, plus sort options, pagination, fuzzy matching (e.g., “Chevy” matching “Chevrolet”), and synonym expansion maintained by admins. The UI highlights trending searches for quicker re-entry and shows immediate feedback such as “0 matches, broaden your price range” and saved filter presets per user. Shoppers add vehicles or add-on services (inspection package, extended warranty offered by the site) to a cart and proceed through checkout where orders may split or merge based on supplier, warehouse location, or promised turnaround (e.g., same-day paperwork vs. 3-business-day delivery). Dealership staff upload documents like titles, buyer’s orders, and inspection PDFs, while role-based permissions control who can read, download, edit, delete, share, submit, or approve each document; inheritance applies by dealership, with explicit overrides for sensitive deals. Finance reviewers see invoice previews with sales tax rates by state/county configured offline, and admins can run A/B tests on listing layouts or checkout steps with clear rollback controls. The backend uses Express to provide REST-style APIs to the React client over the local network, enforcing validation, state transitions, and traceability, while MongoDB persists listings, orders, documents, consent records, audit logs, analytics events, and reconciliation ledgers. Search performance is supported by cached query results (e.g., 10-minute TTL) and a local trending-keyword table updated hourly; pagination must return consistent results per sort key to avoid duplicates. Order processing is implemented as a state machine (Created, Reserved, Invoiced, Settled, Fulfilled, Cancelled) with idempotent actions and exception rollback; if payment or inventory reservation fails, the system reverts within 5 seconds and records the reason. Payments and settlement operate fully offline using an internal wallet ledger and offline methods (cash, cashier’s check, in-house financing); a pluggable adapter interface may exist but must remain disabled unless explicitly enabled in an online deployment. Reconciliation runs nightly to match orders, invoices, and settlements, creating discrepancy tickets for manual review. Privacy controls classify data, mask sensitive fields on screen (e.g., show only last 4 of a driver’s license), encrypt sensitive values at rest using AES-256 with rotated keys, sign API requests with HMAC plus 5-minute anti-replay timestamps, and validate uploaded files by type/size limits (e.g., PDF/JPG/PNG under 10 MB) and hashing with quarantine-on-mismatch; users can view consent history, export their data to a local file, or request account deletion with a 30-day retention hold for financial records.
